title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Seven: In which a scarecrow prevents Sophie from leaving the castle (Fragment 1)
text:
HowlвАЩs Moving Castle

Chapter Seven

I N WHICH A SCARECROW PREVENTS S OPHIE FROM LEAVING THE C ASTLE

Only a particularly bad attack of aches and pains prevented Sophie from setting out for Market Chipping that evening. But the drizzle in Porthaven had got into her bones. She lay in her cubbyhole and ached and worried about Martha. It might not be so bad, she thought. She only had to tell Martha that the suitor she was not sure about was none other than Wizard Howl. That would scare Martha off. And she would tell Martha that the way to scare Howl off was to announce that she was in love with him, and then perhaps to threaten him with aunts.

Sophie was still creaking when she got up next morning. вАЬ Curse the Witch of the Waste!вАЭ she muttered to her stick as she got it out, ready to leave. She could hear Howl singing in the bathroom as if he had never had a tantrum in his life. She tiptoed to the door as fast as she could hobble.

Howl of course came out of the bathroom before she reached it. Sophie looked at him sourly. He was all spruce and dashing, scented gently with apple blossom. The sunlight from the window dazzled off his gray-and-scarlet suit and made a faintly pink halo of his hair.

вАЬI think my hair looks rather good this color,вАЭ he said.

вАЬDo you indeed?вАЭ grumped Sophie.

вАЬIt goes with this suit,вАЭ said Howl. вАЬYou have quite a touch with your needle, donвАЩt you? YouвАЩve given the suit more style somehow.вАЭ

вАЬHuh!вАЭ said Sophie.

Howl stopped with his hand on the knob above the door. вАЬAches and pains troubling you?вАЭ he said. вАЬOr has something annoyed you?вАЭ

вАЬAnnoyed?вАЭ said Sophie. вАЬWhy should I be annoyed? Someone only filled the castle with rotten aspic, and deafened everyone in Porthaven, and scared Calcifer to a cinder, and broke a few hundred hearts. Why should that annoy me?вАЭ

Howl laughed. вАЬI apologize,вАЭ he said, turning the knob to red-down. вАЬThe King wants to see me today. I shall probably be kicking my heels in the Palace until evening, but I can do something for your rheumatism when I get back. DonвАЩt forget to tell Michael I left that spell for him on the bench.вАЭ He smiled sunnily at Sophie and stepped out among the spires of Kingsbury.

вАЬAnd you think that makes it all right!вАЭ Sophie growled as the door shut. But the smile had mollified her. вАЬIf that smile works on me, then itвАЩs no wonder poor Martha doesnвАЩt know her own mind!вАЭ she muttered.

вАЬI need another log before you go,вАЭ Calcifer reminded her.

Sophie hobbled to drop another log into the grate. Then she set off to the door again. But here Michael came running downstairs and snatched the remains of a loaf off the bench as he ran to the door. вАЬYou donвАЩt mind, do you?вАЭ he said in an agitated way. вАЬIвАЩll bring a fresh loaf when I come back. IвАЩve got something very urgent to see to today, but IвАЩll be back by evening. If the sea captain calls for his wind spell, itвАЩs on the end of the bench, clearly labeled.вАЭ He turned the doorknob green-downward and jumped out onto the windy hillside, loaf clutched to his stomach. вАЬSee you!вАЭ he shouted as the castle trundled away past him and the door slammed.

вАЬBotheration!вАЭ said Sophie. вАЬCalcifer, how does a person open the door when thereвАЩs no one inside the castle?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll open it for you, or Michael. Howl does it himself,вАЭ said Calcifer.

So no one would be locked out when Sophie left. She was not at all sure she would be coming back, but she did not intend to tell Calcifer. She gave Michael time to get well on the way to wherever he was going and set off for the door again. This time Calcifer stopped her.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Seven: In which a scarecrow prevents Sophie from leaving the castle

vocabulary.definition: feeling or appearing troubled or nervous.
vocabulary.example: He said in an agitated way.

vocabulary.definition: to make a public or formal declaration about something.
vocabulary.example: The way to scare Howl off was to announce that she was in love with him.

vocabulary.definition: to make someone a little angry or irritated.
vocabulary.example: Why should I be annoyed? Someone only filled the castle with rotten aspic.

vocabulary.definition: to express regret for something that one has done wrong.
vocabulary.example: Howl laughed. вАЬI apologize,вАЭ he said.

vocabulary.definition: a dish in which ingredients are set into a gelatin made from meat stock or broth.
vocabulary.example: Someone only filled the castle with rotten aspic.

vocabulary.definition: a mild expression of annoyance or frustration.
vocabulary.example: вАЬBotheration!вАЭ said Sophie.

vocabulary.definition: a small piece of partly burnt coal or wood that has stopped producing flames but still has combustible matter in it.
vocabulary.example: They scared Calcifer to a cinder.

vocabulary.definition: a small, snug, or secluded space or compartment where someone can sit or hide.
vocabulary.example: She lay in her cubbyhole and ached and worried about Martha.

vocabulary.definition: attractive in a confident, stylish, and charming way.
vocabulary.example: He was all spruce and dashing, scented gently with apple blossom.

vocabulary.definition: to blind temporarily with a bright light; to impress deeply or overwhelm.
vocabulary.example: The sunlight from the window dazzled off his gray-and-scarlet suit.

vocabulary.definition: to make someone unable to hear or make a loud noise that causes temporary hearing loss.
vocabulary.example: They deafened everyone in Porthaven.

vocabulary.definition: light rain falling in very fine drops.
vocabulary.example: But the drizzle in Porthaven had got into her bones.

vocabulary.definition: a frame of metal bars for holding a fire or coals in a fireplace.
vocabulary.example: Sophie hobbled to drop another log into the grate.

vocabulary.definition: a circle of light shown around or above the head of a holy person; a luminous ring.
vocabulary.example: The sunlight made a faintly pink halo of his hair.

vocabulary.definition: to walk with difficulty, especially because of pain or injury.
vocabulary.example: She tiptoed to the door as fast as she could hobble.

vocabulary.definition: to appease someone's anger or anxiety; to soothe.
vocabulary.example: But the smile had mollified her.

vocabulary.definition: a small, slender, pointed tool used in sewing or embroidery.
vocabulary.example: You have quite a touch with your needle, donвАЩt you?

vocabulary.definition: to a higher degree than usual; especially.
vocabulary.example: Only a particularly bad attack of aches and pains prevented Sophie from setting out for Market Chipping that evening.

vocabulary.definition: a disease marked by inflammation and pain in the joints, muscles, or fibrous tissue.
vocabulary.example: I shall probably be kicking my heels in the Palace until evening, but I can do something for your rheumatism when I get back.

vocabulary.definition: to quickly seize something in a rude or eager way.
vocabulary.example: Michael came running downstairs and snatched the remains of a loaf off the bench.

vocabulary.definition: a word or formula believed to have magical effect when spoken or written.
vocabulary.example: I left that spell for him on the bench.

vocabulary.definition: a tapering conical or pyramidal structure on the top of a building, typically a church tower.
vocabulary.example: He stepped out among the spires of Kingsbury.

vocabulary.definition: neat in appearance, well-dressed.
vocabulary.example: He was all spruce and dashing, scented gently with apple blossom.

vocabulary.definition: a man who pursues a relationship with a particular woman, with a view to marriage.
vocabulary.example: She only had to tell Martha that the suitor she was not sure about was none other than Wizard Howl.

vocabulary.definition: an uncontrolled outburst of anger and frustration, typically in a child.
vocabulary.example: She could hear Howl singing in the bathroom as if he had never had a tantrum in his life.

vocabulary.definition: to walk quietly and carefully with one's heels raised and weight on the toes, in order not to be heard or noticed.
vocabulary.example: She tiptoed to the door as fast as she could hobble.

vocabulary.definition: to move or roll somewhere slowly and noisily.
vocabulary.example: The castle trundled away past him.
